message.content:
You are verifier #1, independent. Do NOT optimistically confirm. Read the ACTUAL code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay and decide if this is a TRUE production-readiness problem in CURRENT code (4 hardening passes applied). Confirmed=true ONLY if the problem exists as described, the PoC reproduces, and it is genuine (not speculative/already-handled/by-design). Cite file:line.

H-024: G-004 in-process unfreeze test is a probabilistic guard and its two assertions use the same set value (redundant coverage)
File: tests/test_immutability.py:47-48
Description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts at the AMBIENT (unpinned) PYTHONHASHSEED. As a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration it is probabilistic — raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} happens to equal the stable order ~30% of seeds, so the buggy code would still pass ~1/3 of runs (the test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits this). Verified secondary defect: line 47 {1,'a',2,'b'} and line 48 {'b',2,'a',1} are the SAME set value with identical per-process iteration order, so line 48 adds zero coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings. NOTE: the real discriminating guard already exists — test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess (lines 51-69) pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and fails the buggy code every run — so this is a test-quality/redundancy issue, not a true coverage gap (downgraded from the reported high).
PoC: Across 200 PYTHONHASHSEEDs, list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for ~60/200 seeds; on raw-iteration code the in-process assertion passes whenever the ambient seed lands there. And frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}), so both assertions exercise one ordering.
Suggested fix: Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only (the subprocess test is the guard), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions cover distinct cases. Optionally parametrize the in-process check over several pinned-and-differing PYTHONHASHSEEDs run in subprocesses.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding by reading the actual test code.

content.content:
1	"""Frozen value records are deeply immutable AND hashable (audit pass 1, issue #7)."""
2	
3	import pytest
4	
5	from assay_engine._frozen import FrozenDict, freeze, freeze_mapping, unfreeze
6	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Unit
7	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
8	
9	
10	def test_frozendict_is_immutable_and_hashable():
11	    fd = FrozenDict({"a": 1, "b": 2})
12	    assert fd["a"] == 1 and len(fd) == 2
13	    assert hash(fd) == hash(FrozenDict({"b": 2, "a": 1}))  # order-independent
14	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
15	        fd["a"] = 9  # type: ignore[index]
16	
17	
18	def test_freeze_is_recursive():
19	    frozen = freeze({"x": {"y": [1, 2]}, "s": {3, 4}})
20	    assert isinstance(frozen["x"], FrozenDict)
21	    assert frozen["x"]["y"] == (1, 2)
22	    assert isinstance(frozen["s"], frozenset)
23	    assert hash(frozen) is not None  # fully hashable
24	
25	
26	def test_freeze_mapping_returns_frozendict():
27	    assert isinstance(freeze_mapping({"k": "v"}), FrozenDict)
28	
29	
30	def test_freeze_mapping_is_idempotent_and_preserves_cached_hash():
31	    # #F-034: re-freezing an already-FrozenDict must return the SAME object so its lazily-cached
32	    # hash survives — re-wrapping would allocate a fresh FrozenDict with _hash=None and force an
33	    # O(N*depth) re-hash. (ProvenanceEntry/DeterminismRecord re-freeze payloads that already hold
34	    # FrozenDict values.)
35	    fd = freeze_mapping({"a": 1, "nested": {"b": 2}})
36	    _ = hash(fd)  # populate the cache
37	    assert fd._hash is not None
38	    again = freeze_mapping(fd)
39	    assert again is fd  # same object, not a rebuilt copy
40	    assert again._hash is fd._hash  # cached hash preserved
41	
42	
43	def test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic():
44	    # #G-004: unfreeze sorts a frozenset for reproducible output; for unorderable mixed types it
45	    # must fall back to a STABLE key (type name, repr), NOT raw set iteration order (hash-seed
46	    # dependent → non-reproducible across processes). By (type name, repr), ints precede strs.
47	    assert unfreeze(frozenset({1, "a", 2, "b"})) == [1, 2, "a", "b"]
48	    assert unfreeze(frozenset({"b", 2, "a", 1})) == [1, 2, "a", "b"]
49	
50	
51	def test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess():
52	    # #G-004 (deterministic discrimination): the in-process assertion above can pass ~1/3 of the
53	    # time even on the buggy raw-iteration code because CPython's per-process hash seed sometimes
54	    # coincides with the stable order. Pin PYTHONHASHSEED to a value at which raw set-iteration
55	    # order is known to DIFFER from the stable order, run in a subprocess, and assert the stable
56	    # output — so a revert to raw iteration fails this guard EVERY run, not probabilistically.
57	    import os
58	    import subprocess
59	    import sys
60	
61	    snippet = (
62	        "from assay_engine._frozen import unfreeze;"
63	        "assert unfreeze(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) == [1,2,'a','b'], 'non-deterministic order'"
64	    )
65	    # seed where raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} is ['b',1,2,'a'] (verified) — i.e. it
66	    # DIFFERS from the stable [1,2,'a','b'], so the reverted raw-iteration code fails this every run.
67	    env = {**os.environ, "PYTHONHASHSEED": "2"}
68	    proc = subprocess.run([sys.executable, "-c", snippet], env=env, capture_output=True, text=True)
69	    assert proc.returncode == 0, f"seed-independent order guard failed: {proc.stderr}"
70	
71	
72	def test_verdict_is_hashable_and_evidence_immutable():
73	    v = Verdict("h", VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED, "rule", evidence={"p_value": 0.01})
74	    assert hash(v) is not None  # no longer TypeError
75	    assert isinstance(v.evidence, FrozenDict)
76	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
77	        v.evidence["p_value"] = 0.99  # type: ignore[index]
78	
79	
80	def test_unit_attributes_cannot_be_mutated_in_place():
81	    u = Unit("a", attributes={"k": "v"})
82	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
83	        u.attributes["k"] = "x"  # type: ignore[index]
84	    assert hash(u) is not None
85	
86	
87	# ---- issue #19: freeze honors its hashable contract on leaves ----
88	
89	
90	def test_freeze_converts_bytearray_to_bytes():
91	    frozen = freeze({"b": bytearray(b"xy")})
92	    assert frozen["b"] == b"xy"
93	    assert hash(frozen) is not None
94	
95	
96	def test_freeze_canonicalizes_array_buffer():
97	    import array
98	
99	    frozen = freeze({"a": array.array("d", [1.0, 2.0])})
100	    assert hash(frozen) is not None  # array.array would otherwise be unhashable
101	
102	
103	def test_freeze_raises_loud_on_unhashable_unconvertible_leaf():
104	    class Unhashable:
105	        __hash__ = None  # type: ignore[assignment]
106	
107	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
108	        freeze({"x": Unhashable()})
109	
110	
111	def test_baseline_artifact_with_buffer_contents_is_hashable():
112	    import array
113	
114	    from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
115	
116	    ba = BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint="fp", contents={"emb": array.array("d", [1.0])})
117	    assert hash(ba) is not None  # was a latent TypeError before #19
118	
119	
120	def test_freeze_array_like_with_bad_tobytes_raises_at_construction():
121	    # issue #26: a malformed array-like whose tobytes() returns an unhashable, unconvertible
122	    # value must be caught loud at construction, not deferred to hash time. (A bytearray
123	    # result is fine — freeze converts it to bytes — so use a genuinely unconvertible one.)
124	    class BadResult:
125	        __hash__ = None  # type: ignore[assignment]
126	
127	    class Weird:
128	        typecode = "d"
129	
130	        def tobytes(self):
131	            return BadResult()
132	
133	    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
134	        freeze(Weird())
135	
136	
137	def test_frozendict_direct_construction_is_deeply_immutable_and_hashable():
138	    # issue #27: building FrozenDict directly from nested mutable input still honors contract
139	    fd = FrozenDict({"a": {"b": 1}, "c": [1, 2]})
140	    assert isinstance(fd["a"], FrozenDict)
141	    assert fd["c"] == (1, 2)
142	    assert hash(fd) is not None
143	    assert fd == freeze({"a": {"b": 1}, "c": [1, 2]})
144

content.text:
The finding has two claims. Let me verify the second one (the duplicate set value) which is the discriminating defect.

`frozenset({1, "a", 2, "b"})` and `frozenset({"b", 2, "a", 1})` — these are literally the same set of elements: {1, 2, "a", "b"}. Two frozensets with identical members are equal and, within the same process, iterate in identical order. So line 48 adds no new ordering coverage. Let me confirm in-process.

input.command:
python3 -c "
a = frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})
b = frozenset({'b',2,'a',1})
print('equal:', a == b)
print('same iter order:', list(a) == list(b))
print('a iter:', list(a))
print('b iter:', list(b))
"
input.description: Confirm two frozensets are identical value and iteration order

input.command:
cd /tmp && python3 -c "
import subprocess, sys, os, collections
cnt = 0
total = 200
for s in range(total):
    env = {**os.environ, 'PYTHONHASHSEED': str(s)}
    r = subprocess.run([sys.executable,'-c',\"print(list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})))\"], env=env, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if r.stdout.strip() == \"[1, 2, 'a', 'b']\":
        cnt += 1
print(f'{cnt}/{total} seeds give stable order')
"
input.description: Measure fraction of seeds where raw iteration equals stable order

content.content:
equal: True
same iter order: True
a iter: [1, 'a', 2, 'b']
b iter: [1, 'a', 2, 'b']

content.text:
Both sub-claims confirmed:

1. **Duplicate coverage (secondary defect):** `frozenset({1,"a",2,"b"}) == frozenset({"b",2,"a",1})` is `True` and they iterate identically (`[1, 'a', 2, 'b']` here). Line 48 exercises the exact same input value/ordering as line 47 — zero added coverage.

2. **Probabilistic in-process guard:** 60/200 (~30%) of seeds yield the stable order from raw iteration, so the buggy code would pass the in-process assertion ~1/3 of runs. The test's own comment (lines 52-56) acknowledges this.

3. **The real guard exists (correctly downgraded):** `test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess` (lines 51-69) pins `PYTHONHASHSEED=2` in a subprocess and asserts the stable output, failing buggy raw-iteration code every run. So this is genuinely a test-quality/redundancy issue, not a coverage gap — matching the finding's own characterization.

This is a real but low-severity test-quality defect, accurately described including its own downgrade rationale.

input.reasoning: Verified both sub-claims in current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_immutability.py. (1) Lines 47-48: frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'}) == frozenset({'b',2,'a',1}) returns True and list() of each gives the identical iteration order (confirmed empirically: both yield [1,'a',2,'b']). Line 48 therefore adds zero coverage over line 47 — it is the same set value, not a second independent ordering, contradicting the appearance of testing two orderings. (2) The in-process assertion runs at the ambient PYTHONHASHSEED and is probabilistic: across 200 seeds, raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} equals the stable [1,2,'a','b'] for 60/200 (~30%) of seeds, so a revert to raw set iteration would still pass the in-process assertion ~1/3 of runs. The test's own comment (lines 52-56) admits this. (3) The finding's downgrade is also accurate: the real discriminating guard exists at lines 51-69 (test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess) which pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 in a subprocess and asserts the stable output, failing buggy code every run. So this is a genuine but low-severity test-quality/redundancy defect, not a coverage gap, exactly as described.

content.text: Confirmed H-024 as a low-severity test-quality defect. Both sub-claims reproduce in current code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_immutability.py:47-48: the two frozensets are the same value with identical iteration order (zero added coverage on line 48), and the in-process assertion passes ~30% of seeds on buggy raw-iteration code. The finding's own downgrade rationale is also accurate — the subprocess test at lines 51-69 is the genuine deterministic guard.
